subject: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)

2026-06-12.patch_summary: This patch introduces a buffer pool feature to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel for servicing requests. This decouples the number of buffers required from the number of entries in the ring, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The patch adds support for a FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and introduces a buffer ring data structure to manage buffer selection and recycling. The server must provide the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first sqe, and the kernel will select and recycle buffers from this pool as needed.

reviews.author: Miklos Szeredi
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the fuse io-uring interface is inconsistent because it decouples buffers from sqes but not registration, and requested that registration be decoupled as well.
reviews.raw_body:
I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Thanks,
Miklos

reviews.author: Miklos Szeredi
reviews.summary: reviewer questioned the mapping of buf_id to buffer address, suggesting it would be simpler to provide the buffer address directly
reviews.raw_body:
How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
patchset).  What about a new directory:
Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

Thanks,
Milklos

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used as needed by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing a concern about queue setup and configuration, proposing to add a new command operation (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) to pass in queue-level settings, including the number of reduced queues, buffer pool, and flags. This would decouple queue creation from sqe registration and provide a clean way to set up queue configurations.
reviews.raw_body:
Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how the server would access specific buffers within the buffer pool, explaining that the server knows the starting address and size of each buffer and can compute the address from the buf_id.

The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how to return the selected buffer in fuse_uring_ent_in_out, proposing to use a buf_id instead of the buffer address and discussing potential future implications.

Author agrees with Miklos Szeredi's suggestion, confirms they will add uAPI documentation and update the directory.
reviews.raw_body:
The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

---

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

---

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Miklos Szeredi
reviews.summary:
reviewer questioned how the kernel selects a specific buffer from multiple pools, noting that this is not handled by the current interface and will become even more complex when fixed ops require a buffer index

reviewer suggested renaming 'pool index' to 'buf_index' to maintain consistency with existing terminology
reviews.raw_body:
But which buffer?   The interface is supposed to handle multiple
pools, in which case the server needs to find the pool and the buffer
within the pool.

FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

---

Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.
So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
"buf_index" for registered buffers?

Thanks,
Miklos


Thanks,
Miklos

reviews.author: Miklos Szeredi
reviews.summary: Reviewer suggested separating buffer pool registration into a separate operation to allow for independent setup and potentially delayed execution.
reviews.raw_body:
Why not a separate op for bufpool registration, so it can be done
independently of the setup, possibly at some later time?

Thanks,
Miklos

reviews.author: Bernd Schubert
reviews.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert expressed concerns that a fixed number of queues and queue entries is rarely optimal, and suggested that the config setting should only prepare the kernel for dynamic routing updates.
sentiment_signals: config setting may not be sufficient
reviews.raw_body:
Can we please discuss this on Thursday? I'm by no means opposed to a
config SQE, but I don't see how it will really help. My issue is that
any fixed number of queues and queue entries is almost never the right one.
The config setting (via SQE, ioctl or FUSE_INIT) could be used to
prepare the kernel, but final routing update needs to be dynamic.


Thanks,
Bernd

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used as needed by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about adding support for multiple buffer pools, explaining that a 'uint16_t pool_idx' would be needed in struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to indicate which pool, and userspace would need to keep track of the starting address of each pool.

The author addressed a concern about how FIXED ops through zero-copy handle buffer indexing, explaining that userspace determines and passes the index to the kernel in the init setup. The author confirmed that no buf index needs to be passed back by the kernel for these operations.

Author acknowledged that the 12-byte limit for buffer pool configuration is due to a uapi definition and agreed to consider increasing it

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about supporting multiple buffer pools by proposing the use of a 'pool_idx' field to indicate which pool to use.

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about how to manage buffer pools, proposing that the kernel pass back a 'pool index' and let userspace keep track of the registered buffer id.
reviews.raw_body:
If we later wanted to add support for multiple buffer pools, we would
need to extend the struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to include a "uint16_t
pool_idx" to indicate which pool. Userspace would be responsible for
keeping track of the starting address of each pool.

---

For FIXED ops not through zero-copy, there's one index for the entire
pool (eg this is the index where the pool resides). It passes this by
setting the sqe->buf_index.
For FIXED ops through zero-copy, userspace is required to register a
sparse buffer where the kernel should place the application's/page
cache's pages. Userspace determines the index into this sparse buffer
for the sqe and it passes this index to the kernel in the init setup
in the "uint16_t zero_copy_index;" field when it registers the sqe.

Neither require the kernel to pass back a buf index corresponding to
the fixed buffer index. For both cases, userspace knows the index for
the fixed buffer (it is the one who determined it) and it is the one
who passes this info to the kernel at setup time.

---

The 12 byte limit comes from the "struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out"
definition in the uapi [1].

---

For multiple buffer pools, I was thinking that would be through a
separate "uint16_t pool_idx" field to indicate which pool.

---

I think we should just pass back "pool index" and have userspace keep
track of the registered buffer id that corresponds to that pool index.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h#L1255

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary: Author Joanne Koong responded to a discussion about needing multiple buffer pools in one queue, stating that she's not convinced it's necessary and thinks incremental buffer consumption would address the use cases.
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
[1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
be simpler.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/CAJnrk1Yw3=z7W_my4pLG6avBeDZkUp3j1LZk-RVpGv2vAsw-ZA@mail.gmail.com/

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author agrees that dynamic queue/ent capacity adjustment is a useful feature, but suggests implementing it as a separate command operation after io-uring setup to simplify setup logic and avoid potential races.

Author acknowledged the need for a dynamic final routing update, agreeing it should be handled dynamically.
sentiment_signals: acknowledged the usefulness of dynamic resizing
reviews.raw_body:
I agree. I definitely think it would be useful to add more queue/ent
capacity dynamically, but imo I think that should be done separately
through a new cmd op after uring setup. I think it'd save some
headache to keep setup and runtime resizing as separate concerns /
paths and would be easier to reason about different races that could
happen and would keep the setup logic simpler.

---

Agreed that final routing update would be dynamic.

Thanks,
Joanne

reviews.author: Miklos Szeredi
reviews.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi expressed confusion about the patch's intention regarding buffer pools per ring, specifically questioning whether it supports multiple bufpools of different sizes or not.
reviews.raw_body:
Confused.  I thought this was exactly about multiple bufpools
(containing different size bufs) per ring.

If not, then what's the point of dynamically assigning bufs to sqe's?

Thanks,
Miklos

reviews.author: Bernd Schubert
reviews.summary: The reviewer expressed confusion about how to implement queues with different IO sizes and questioned the logic of selecting a queue, suggesting starting with a single max-IO buf per numa node and scaling up as needed.
reviews.raw_body:
To be honest, I have no idea how to implement queues with different IO
sizes. What would be the logic to select a queue? And what is if we
start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
scale up based on need? Let's say we have:

Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
now additionally we get for the same CPU

- multiple queues of the same buf size
- multiple queues of different buf sizes


Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
anymore.

For me this does not make any sense and to say it with your words "I do
not see a compelling use case for single bufpool in one queue".


Thanks,
Bernd

This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.
